segments.title: A plausible Soviet capital ship for 1935
segments.summary: The design exercise asks what a Soviet battleship programme might have produced by 1935, using imported ideas and a shipyard-friendly hull.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke begins with a Soviet-versus-German scenario and tries to make the simulated ship resemble the interwar Soviet naval programme rather than an idealised super-battleship. The exercise exposes how hull form, turret placement, propulsion, armour, and foreign technical assistance constrain a credible design. His first attempt also shows the limits of using a game’s available parts to represent a historically distinctive superstructure.

evidence.note: He sets a 1935 Soviet battleship scenario and begins selecting the hull and 16-inch main armament.
He describes the result as a quick Soviet Naval Rod design.

segments.title: Why Soviet naval NCOs mattered
segments.summary: A brief aside links Soviet naval effectiveness to professional NCOs who retained practical experience below a frequently changing leadership.
segments.body: In answering why the simulated Soviet crews should not simply be treated as recruits, Dr. Clarke distinguishes senior-level turnover from continuity in lower and middle ranks. He argues that the navy retained an unusually professional NCO cadre compared with other parts of the Soviet armed forces. The point is a useful reminder that personnel systems and institutional memory can matter as much as a fleet’s headline equipment.

evidence.note: He contrasts turnover at the top with the professional NCO force that retained Soviet naval institutional memory.

segments.title: Ambition versus a buildable Soviet battle fleet
segments.summary: The completed design becomes a way to separate Soviet paper ambitions from the smaller number of capital ships industry might plausibly have delivered.
segments.body: The simulated Soviet ship carries nine 16-inch guns, substantial secondary armament, torpedoes, and a broad defensive fit. Dr. Clarke argues that a programme begun in 1930 might plausibly have delivered two or three such ships by the Second World War with British and Italian support, rather than the twenty ships Soviet planning envisaged. The contrast turns a design sketch into a question of industrial capacity, technical imports, and realistic procurement scale.

evidence.note: He lists the design’s weapons and argues that two or three, not twenty, might have been achievable with foreign support.

segments.title: Why Scharnhorst was not a battlecruiser
segments.summary: Gun calibre and speed do not settle a ship’s classification: the Scharnhorsts were built as fast battleships within German industrial limits.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke rejects the common shortcut of calling Scharnhorst and Gneisenau battlecruisers because of their speed and 11-inch armament. He notes that broadside gun count is not a reliable dividing line, since some battlecruisers mounted more guns than some battleships. In his account, the 11-inch battery reflected what Germany could sustain while its naval industry was recovering, not a deliberate decision to build a lesser battlecruiser type.

evidence.note: He argues that Scharnhorst and Gneisenau were built as fast battleships and explains the industrial context of their 11-inch guns.

segments.title: A 15-inch alternative to King George V’s armament
segments.summary: The second design test imagines an improved Royal Navy 15-inch gun and considers how it might have changed the King George V class.
segments.body: Moving from the Soviet experiment to a Royal Navy scenario, Dr. Clarke explores a hypothetical 15-inch/52 weapon rather than the historical 14-inch battery. He suggests that existing experience with large triple turrets could have eased a 15-inch triple-turret arrangement and avoided some of the King George V class’s turret difficulties. The test remains counterfactual, but it frames calibre choice through range, shell performance, production maturity, speed, armour, and the trade-off between a smaller number of larger guns and a heavier broadside.

evidence.note: He outlines a hypothetical British 15-inch/52 development and its possible use in King George V-style triple turrets.
The battle result is offered as support for the counterfactual enhanced 15-inch-gun argument.
